## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Mechanical Rust pace. Replace dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` with `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Register four calibrant fixtures with deterministic verdicts; pin behavior in `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Delete `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` and its `lib.rs` declaration; add `rbtdrl_calibrant` module + `rbtdtl_calibrant` test module.
  - Four fixtures registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` (fixture-name consts), `rbtdrc_fixture_disposition`, and `rbtdrc_sections_for_fixture`:
    - `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent): 4 cases — `pass`, `fail`, `skip`, `pass_with_output` (writes `output.txt`).
    - `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent): two sections — §A `[pass, fail, not_reached]` (intra-section), §B `[not_reached]` (inter-section).
    - `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing): 2 cases — case 1 probe `Ok` then `Pass`; case 2 probe hardcoded `Err` (case body unreachable).
    - `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent): single case writes `ran.sentinel` into its case `dir`.
  - All four `rbtdrm_required_colophons` entries: `Some(&[])` (calibrant cases never shell out).
  - `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` unit tests pin per-case verdict, sentinel write/non-write, disposition tags, sections registration, manifest entries.
  
  ## Locked decisions
  
  - **Case-written file**: `output.txt` — distinct from engine-auto `trace.txt`. Only `pass_with_output` writes it; engine writes `trace.txt` for every case implicitly.
  - **Sentinel mechanism**: not_reached cases and `calibrant-sentinel` write `ran.sentinel` into their case temp dir (`dir.join("ran.sentinel")`). Bash side (Pace 2) asserts presence/absence by globbing under the `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` it set for the rbtd invocation. No project-relative paths — hygiene matters.
  - **Probe-Err mechanism**: `calibrant-progressing` case 2's `check` fn is hardcoded `Err`. No env-var or file coupling. Single fixture run exercises both `Ok` and `Err` probe paths deterministically.
  - **Suite is bash composition**: no `RBTDRM_SUITE_CALIBRANT` constant. Suite-level fail-fast comes from `rbte_engine.sh` `set -euo pipefail` per existing pattern. Suite tabtarget (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.calibrant.sh`) defers to Pace 2 alongside its testbench consumer.
  - **No per-fixture tabtargets**: calibrant fixtures are framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench (Pace 2) invokes the `rbtd` binary directly — tabtargets are thin shells not part of the contract under test. Deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern; justified because pristine/canonical are operator-facing, calibrant isn't.
  - **`rbtdrd_dummy.rs` full delete**: no consumers reference it; calibrant supersedes the placeholder. Git history preserves the file.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Bash testbench, case files, zipper entry, suite tabtarget, runbook entry — all Pace 2 (₢BBAAi).
  - New engine concepts beyond what BBAAd landed.
  - Cross-kit composition.
  
  ## Depends on
  
  BBAAd — `rbtdre_Disposition`, `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast`, `rbtdrb_Probe` already in place.

Two new tabtarget launchers (rbtd-r.Run.canonical-establish.sh, rbtd-s.SingleCase.canonical-establish.sh) — bash side is fixture-name agnostic, no zipper enrollment needed. 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
75	
76	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
77	
78	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
79	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
80	
81	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
82	
83	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
84	
85	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
86	
87	## Suite-level fail-fast
88	
89	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
90	
91	## No-teardown decision
92	
93	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
94	
95	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
96	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
97	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
98	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
99	
100	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
101	
102	## Operator scope
103	
104	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.
105	
106	## Coupling to ₣A_
107	
108	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
109	
110	## Locked design decisions — pointers
111	
112	- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
113	- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
114	
115	## Bash blackbox calibrant — design
116	
117	A bash-driven black-box test layer over the Theurge binary, driving synthetic fixtures (the "calibrant" family) with deterministic verdicts to verify the operator-facing surface of the test framework: CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fixture and suite fail-fast, and the disposition × keep-going policy gate landed in BBAAd. Rust unit tests verify engine functions in isolation; the calibrant layer verifies what the operator sees when the binary runs.
118	
119	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
120	
121	**With-the-grain composition.** Tabtargets are bare exec stubs; orchestration lives in zippers/workbenches/testbenches/suites. The bash blackbox is its own testbench (`Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh`) registered through `rbz_zipper.sh` with its own colophon and tabtarget. BUK's self-test stays untouched. No "BUK + calibrant" composite tabtarget — operator sequences `buw-st` then the new colophon, same shape as the existing rbtd test-suite tier ladder.
122	
123	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
124	
125	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
126	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
127	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
128	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification (placed after the failing fixture in the calibrant suite; bash asserts sentinel absent).
129	
130	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
131	
132	**Bash case catalog** (`Tools/rbk/rbts/`, 14 cases across 6 sections):
133	
134	- `verdict-propagation` (4) — pass/fail/skip exit codes + trace file
135	- `fixture-fail-fast` (2) — intra-section, inter-section
136	- `disposition-policy` (3) — Independent+keep-going runs all; StateProgressing+keep-going refused with policy stderr; StateProgressing default runs fail-fast
137	- `probe-diagnostics` (1) — unmet-probe stderr contains "precondition '%s' not met:" + "remediation:"
138	- `suite-fail-fast` (2) — suite aborts on failing fixture; subsequent fixture's sentinel absent
139	- `cli-surface` (2) — unknown fixture errors clearly; missing arg → usage
140	
141	**Two-pace shape:**
142	
143	1. Calibrant fixture foundation (Rust) — module rename, fixtures registered, manifest entries, dispositions tagged, Rust unit tests pin ground truth.
144	2. Calibrant bash blackbox driver (BUK side) — testbench, case files, zipper entry, tabtarget, runbook entry. Depends on (1).
145	
146	**BURV chain is contract, not hook.** The `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` / `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR` override is load-bearing infrastructure already used by the BUT framework's `buto_unit_*` invocations and the Theurge's `rbtdri_invocation` shell-outs (BUS0 §540-552). Per-case BURV isolation, nested through calibrant cases when they invoke `rbtd`, composes cleanly with any operator-set `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` exported in the parent shell. Calibrant cases incidentally regression-test this chain.
147	
148	**Out of scope for both paces:**
149	
150	- Cross-kit testbench composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench).
151	- Orchestrating tabtargets containing exec-of-children logic.
152	- Trace-file format invariants beyond a single `pass_with_output` smoke check.
153	- Color rendering / terminal width contracts.
154	
155	## References
156	
157	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
158	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
159	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
160	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
161	
162	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAh
163	
164	## Character
165	
166	Mechanical Rust pace. Replace dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` with `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Register four calibrant fixtures with deterministic verdicts; pin behavior in `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.
167	
168	## Scope
169	
170	- Delete `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` and its `lib.rs` declaration; add `rbtdrl_calibrant` module + `rbtdtl_calibrant` test module.
171	- Four fixtures registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` (fixture-name consts), `rbtdrc_fixture_disposition`, and `rbtdrc_sections_for_fixture`:
172	  - `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent): 4 cases — `pass`, `fail`, `skip`, `pass_with_output` (writes `output.txt`).
173	  - `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent): two sections — §A `[pass, fail, not_reached]` (intra-section), §B `[not_reached]` (inter-section).
174	  - `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing): 2 cases — case 1 probe `Ok` then `Pass`; case 2 probe hardcoded `Err` (case body unreachable).
175	  - `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent): single case writes `ran.sentinel` into its case `dir`.
176	- All four `rbtdrm_required_colophons` entries: `Some(&[])` (calibrant cases never shell out).
177	- `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` unit tests pin per-case verdict, sentinel write/non-write, disposition tags, sections registration, manifest entries.
178	
179	## Locked decisions
180	
181	- **Case-written file**: `output.txt` — distinct from engine-auto `trace.txt`. Only `pass_with_output` writes it; engine writes `trace.txt` for every case implicitly.
182	- **Sentinel mechanism**: not_reached cases and `calibrant-sentinel` write `ran.sentinel` into their case temp dir (`dir.join("ran.sentinel")`). Bash side (Pace 2) asserts presence/absence by globbing under the `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` it set for the rbtd invocation. No project-relative paths — hygiene matters.
183	- **Probe-Err mechanism**: `calibrant-progressing` case 2's `check` fn is hardcoded `Err`. No env-var or file coupling. Single fixture run exercises both `Ok` and `Err` probe paths deterministically.
184	- **Suite is bash composition**: no `RBTDRM_SUITE_CALIBRANT` constant. Suite-level fail-fast comes from `rbte_engine.sh` `set -euo pipefail` per existing pattern. Suite tabtarget (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.calibrant.sh`) defers to Pace 2 alongside its testbench consumer.
185	- **No per-fixture tabtargets**: calibrant fixtures are framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench (Pace 2) invokes the `rbtd` binary directly — tabtargets are thin shells not part of the contract under test. Deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern; justified because pristine/canonical are operator-facing, calibrant isn't.
186	- **`rbtdrd_dummy.rs` full delete**: no consumers reference it; calibrant supersedes the placeholder. Git history preserves the file.
187	
188	## Out of scope
189	
190	- Bash testbench, case files, zipper entry, suite tabtarget, runbook entry — all Pace 2 (₢BBAAi).
191	- New engine concepts beyond what BBAAd landed.
192	- Cross-kit composition.
193	
194	## Depends on
195	
196	BBAAd — `rbtdre_Disposition`, `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast`, `rbtdrb_Probe` already in place.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — mimics operator onboarding journey under theurge control)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: crucible suite)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. Runbook lives in README.md release section.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions — pointers

- **Depot identity collapse** (BBAAM, landed): RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming** (BBAAN/BBAAQ/BBAAR, landed): SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.

## Bash blackbox calibrant — design

A bash-driven black-box test layer over the Theurge binary, driving synthetic fixtures (the "calibrant" family) with deterministic verdicts to verify the operator-facing surface of the test framework: CLI exit codes, stderr diagnostic format, fixture and suite fail-fast, and the disposition × keep-going policy gate landed in BBAAd. Rust unit tests verify engine functions in isolation; the calibrant layer verifies what the operator sees when the binary runs.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**With-the-grain composition.** Tabtargets are bare exec stubs; orchestration lives in zippers/workbenches/testbenches/suites. The bash blackbox is its own testbench (`Tools/rbk/rbtt_testbench.sh`) registered through `rbz_zipper.sh` with its own colophon and tabtarget. BUK's self-test stays untouched. No "BUK + calibrant" composite tabtarget — operator sequences `buw-st` then the new colophon, same shape as the existing rbtd test-suite tier ladder.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification (placed after the failing fixture in the calibrant suite; bash asserts sentinel absent).

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

**Bash case catalog** (`Tools/rbk/rbts/`, 14 cases across 6 sections):

- `verdict-propagation` (4) — pass/fail/skip exit codes + trace file
- `fixture-fail-fast` (2) — intra-section, inter-section
- `disposition-policy` (3) — Independent+keep-going runs all; StateProgressing+keep-going refused with policy stderr; StateProgressing default runs fail-fast
- `probe-diagnostics` (1) — unmet-probe stderr contains "precondition '%s' not met:" + "remediation:"
- `suite-fail-fast` (2) — suite aborts on failing fixture; subsequent fixture's sentinel absent
- `cli-surface` (2) — unknown fixture errors clearly; missing arg → usage

**Two-pace shape:**

1. Calibrant fixture foundation (Rust) — module rename, fixtures registered, manifest entries, dispositions tagged, Rust unit tests pin ground truth.
2. Calibrant bash blackbox driver (BUK side) — testbench, case files, zipper entry, tabtarget, runbook entry. Depends on (1).

**BURV chain is contract, not hook.** The `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` / `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR` override is load-bearing infrastructure already used by the BUT framework's `buto_unit_*` invocations and the Theurge's `rbtdri_invocation` shell-outs (BUS0 §540-552). Per-case BURV isolation, nested through calibrant cases when they invoke `rbtd`, composes cleanly with any operator-set `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` exported in the parent shell. Calibrant cases incidentally regression-test this chain.

**Out of scope for both paces:**

- Cross-kit testbench composition (BUK fixtures registered in rbk testbench).
- Orchestrating tabtargets containing exec-of-children logic.
- Trace-file format invariants beyond a single `pass_with_output` smoke check.
- Color rendering / terminal width contracts.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAAh

## Character

Mechanical Rust pace. Replace dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` with `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` per RCG terminal exclusivity — `c` is owned by crucible). Register four calibrant fixtures with deterministic verdicts; pin behavior in `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Self-contained — bash driver pace consumes this but does not land here.

## Scope

- Delete `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` and its `lib.rs` declaration; add `rbtdrl_calibrant` module + `rbtdtl_calibrant` test module.
- Four fixtures registered in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` (fixture-name consts), `rbtdrc_fixture_disposition`, and `rbtdrc_sections_for_fixture`:
  - `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent): 4 cases — `pass`, `fail`, `skip`, `pass_with_output` (writes `output.txt`).
  - `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent): two sections — §A `[pass, fail, not_reached]` (intra-section), §B `[not_reached]` (inter-section).
  - `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing): 2 cases — case 1 probe `Ok` then `Pass`; case 2 probe hardcoded `Err` (case body unreachable).
  - `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent): single case writes `ran.sentinel` into its case `dir`.
- All four `rbtdrm_required_colophons` entries: `Some(&[])` (calibrant cases never shell out).
- `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs` unit tests pin per-case verdict, sentinel write/non-write, disposition tags, sections registration, manifest entries.

## Locked decisions

- **Case-written file**: `output.txt` — distinct from engine-auto `trace.txt`. Only `pass_with_output` writes it; engine writes `trace.txt` for every case implicitly.
- **Sentinel mechanism**: not_reached cases and `calibrant-sentinel` write `ran.sentinel` into their case temp dir (`dir.join("ran.sentinel")`). Bash side (Pace 2) asserts presence/absence by globbing under the `BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR` it set for the rbtd invocation. No project-relative paths — hygiene matters.
- **Probe-Err mechanism**: `calibrant-progressing` case 2's `check` fn is hardcoded `Err`. No env-var or file coupling. Single fixture run exercises both `Ok` and `Err` probe paths deterministically.
- **Suite is bash composition**: no `RBTDRM_SUITE_CALIBRANT` constant. Suite-level fail-fast comes from `rbte_engine.sh` `set -euo pipefail` per existing pattern. Suite tabtarget (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.calibrant.sh`) defers to Pace 2 alongside its testbench consumer.
- **No per-fixture tabtargets**: calibrant fixtures are framework-test plumbing, not operator workflows. The bash testbench (Pace 2) invokes the `rbtd` binary directly — tabtargets are thin shells not part of the contract under test. Deviates from BBAAe's per-fixture pattern; justified because pristine/canonical are operator-facing, calibrant isn't.
- **`rbtdrd_dummy.rs` full delete**: no consumers reference it; calibrant supersedes the placeholder. Git history preserves the file.

## Out of scope

- Bash testbench, case files, zipper entry, suite tabtarget, runbook entry — all Pace 2 (₢BBAAi).
- New engine concepts beyond what BBAAd landed.
- Cross-kit composition.

## Depends on

BBAAd — `rbtdre_Disposition`, `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast`, `rbtdrb_Probe` already in place.

17	// RBTDRB — precondition-probe helper for StateProgressing fixtures
18	//
19	// Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at entry.
20	// If the observed state matches the expected state the case body proceeds;
21	// otherwise the case returns a Fail verdict naming the expected state and
22	// the closest fixture that establishes it. The probe encapsulates state
23	// verification at the case level, so the engine does not need to track
24	// case history to support safe a-la-carte single-case rerun.
25	
26	use crate::rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_Verdict;
27	
28	/// A precondition probe attached to a StateProgressing case.
29	pub struct rbtdrb_Probe {
30	    /// Human-readable label for the precondition (e.g., "depot levied").
31	    pub name: &'static str,
32	    /// Check fn. Returns Ok(()) when the precondition holds; Err carries a
33	    /// diagnostic describing the observed state.
34	    pub check: fn() -> Result<(), String>,
35	    /// Operator-actionable next step naming the closest fixture or tabtarget
36	    /// that establishes the precondition.
37	    pub remediation: &'static str,
38	}
39	
40	/// Assert a probe at case entry.
41	///
42	/// On Ok(()) the precondition holds and the case body should proceed. On
43	/// Err(verdict) the case should return the verdict immediately — the verdict
44	/// names the unmet precondition and the remediation step.
45	pub fn rbtdrb_assert(probe: &rbtdrb_Probe) -> Result<(), rbtdre_Verdict> {
46	    match (probe.check)() {
47	        Ok(()) => Ok(()),
48	        Err(observed) => Err(rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!(
49	            "precondition '{}' not met: {}\nremediation: {}",
50	            probe.name, observed, probe.remediation
51	        ))),
52	    }
53	}

6	
7	# Rust Coding Guide (RCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	RCG is deliberately minimal. Trust Claude's inherent Rust idioms for error handling, struct design, documentation, and general style. This guide covers only project-specific conventions that override or extend standard Rust practice.
12	
13	The primary concern is **minting discipline** — ensuring all identifiers follow the project's prefix naming rules for clarity, grep-ability, and ease of discussion in non-Rust-aware contexts.
14	
15	## Crate Boilerplate
16	
17	Every Rust file that serves as a crate root must include these attributes. This includes `lib.rs`, `main.rs`, and every integration test file in `tests/` (each compiles as a separate crate).
18	
19	```rust
20	#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
21	#![allow(private_interfaces)]
22	#![deny(warnings)]
23	```
24	
25	- `non_camel_case_types`: Allows prefixed type names like `jjrg_Gallops`
26	- `private_interfaces`: Allows z-prefixed (internal) types in public enum variants
27	- `deny(warnings)`: All warnings are build failures — no warning is acceptable in committed code
28	
29	The `allow` directives must appear after `deny(warnings)` to override it for RCG-specific naming patterns. The `deny` directive ensures warnings never accumulate — a warning today becomes a mystery tomorrow.
30	
31	## File Naming
32	
33	### Source Files
34	
35	Pattern: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs`
36	
37	| Component | Description |
38	|-----------|-------------|
39	| `{cipher}` | Project cipher (2-5 chars): `jj`, `vv`, `vo`, `cm`, `bu` |
40	| `r` | Rust source marker |
41	| `{classifier}` | Single letter, name typically starts with this letter |
42	| `{name}` | Descriptive snake_case name |
43	
44	Examples:
45	- `jjrg_gallops.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `g`, "gallops"
46	- `jjrc_core.rs` — JJ Rust, classifier `c`, "core"
47	- `vvcg_guard.rs` — VV Core, classifier `g`, "guard"
48	
49	### Test Files
50	
51	Pattern: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs`
52	
53	Tests use `t` instead of `r` after the cipher, with matching classifier:
54	
55	| Source | Test |
56	|--------|------|
57	| `jjrg_gallops.rs` | `jjtg_gallops.rs` |
58	| `jjrc_core.rs` | `jjtc_core.rs` |
59	| `vvcg_guard.rs` | `vvtg_guard.rs` |
60	
61	Module wiring in `lib.rs`:
62	```rust
63	pub mod jjrg_gallops;
64	
65	#[cfg(test)]
66	mod jjtg_gallops;
67	```
68	
69	## Declaration Naming
70	
71	**All public declarations from a source file must start with that file's full prefix.**
72	
73	This is non-negotiable. It enables grep-based discovery and clear provenance in discussions.
74	
75	### Naming Patterns by Type
76	
77	For a file with prefix `jjrg`:
78	
79	| Declaration | Public Pattern | Example | Internal Pattern | Example |
80	|-------------|---------------|---------|-----------------|---------|
81	| Struct | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_Gallops` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_Output` |
82	| Enum | `{prefix}_PascalName` | `jjrg_PaceState` | `z{prefix}_PascalName` | `zjjrg_ParseMode` |
83	| Function | `{prefix}_snake_name` | `jjrg_load()` | `z{prefix}_snake_name` | `zjjrg_validate()` |
84	| Constant | `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH` | `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` | `ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH` |
85	| Trait | `{prefix}_TraitName` | `jjrg_Storable` | `z{prefix}_TraitName` | `zjjrg_Parseable` |
86	| Type alias | `{prefix}_AliasName` | `jjrg_Result` | `z{prefix}_AliasName` | `zjjrg_InternalResult` |
87	
88	### Impl Methods
89	
90	Methods on prefixed types also carry the prefix:
91	
92	```rust
93	impl jjrg_Gallops {
94	    pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<Self, String> { ... }
95	    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
96	}
97	```
98	
99	Rationale: When reading `gallops.jjrg_save(path)`, the provenance is immediately clear. In discussions, "call jjrg_save" is unambiguous.
100	
101	## Internal Declarations
102	
103	**The `z`/`Z` prefix applies uniformly to all internal declaration types** — structs, enums, functions, constants, traits, and type aliases. The prefix letter follows the casing context: lowercase `z` for snake_case and PascalCase names, uppercase `Z` for SCREAMING_SNAKE constants. See the Internal Pattern columns in the naming table above.
104	
105	This parallels BCG's `z«prefix»_` convention for bash. The key difference: BCG also uses `z_«name»` for local variables because bash has flat function scope. Rust's block scoping eliminates this need — local variables use normal names without any z-prefix.
106	
107	Internal items use `pub(crate)` visibility to enable testing from separate test files:
108	
109	```rust
110	pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_silks(s: &str) -> bool {
111	    // Internal helper, testable from jjtg_gallops.rs
112	}
113	
114	pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
115	
116	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
117	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
118	}
119	```
120	
121	The `z`/`Z` prefix signals "do not call from outside this module's conceptual boundary" even though Rust visibility permits crate-internal access.
122	
123	### Visibility and Derive Macros
124	
125	When using derive macros like `clap`, internal argument structs (`z{prefix}_*Args`) may appear in public enum variants. Rust warns about this visibility mismatch. The `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` attribute in Crate Boilerplate suppresses this expected noise — the `z` prefix communicates intent to humans even when Rust forces technical exposure.
126	
127	### Struct Field Visibility for Tests
128	
129	When tests construct internal structs directly, both the struct AND its fields need `pub(crate)` visibility:
130	
131	```rust
132	// ❌ Tests can't construct this — fields are private
133	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
134	    heat_silks: String,
135	    pace_count: Option<u32>,
136	}
137	
138	// ✅ Tests can construct this — fields are accessible
139	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output {
140	    pub(crate) heat_silks: String,
141	    pub(crate) pace_count: Option<u32>,
142	}
143	```
144	
145	If tests only READ internal structs (returned by functions), field visibility doesn't matter. But if tests CONSTRUCT them for unit testing serialization, validation, or helper logic, fields must be `pub(crate)`.
146	
147	## Test Functions
148	
149	Test functions in `jjt{classifier}_{name}.rs` files use the test file's prefix:
150	
151	```rust
152	// In jjtg_gallops.rs
153	
154	use super::jjrg_gallops::*;
155	
156	#[test]
157	fn jjtg_load_empty_file() {
158	    // ...
159	}
160	
161	#[test]
162	fn jjtg_save_creates_parent_dirs() {
163	    // ...
164	}
165	```
166	
167	## Visibility Layering
168	
169	| Level | Visibility | Prefix | Testable |
170	|-------|------------|--------|----------|
171	| Public API | `pub` | `{prefix}_` | Yes |
172	| Internal | `pub(crate)` | `z{prefix}_` | Yes |
173	| Implementation detail | (none) | (rare) | No |
174	
175	Prefer `pub(crate)` with `z` prefix over true private for anything that benefits from testing. Reserve true private for trivial implementation details.
176	
177	## Minting Discipline
178	
179	All naming follows the project's minting rules. Key constraints:
180	
181	1. **Terminal exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both
182	2. **Cipher ownership**: All identifiers derive from their project's cipher
183	3. **Grep-ability**: `grep jjrg_` finds all declarations from that module
184	
185	Reference: CLAUDE.md "Prefix Naming Discipline" section.
186	
187	## Interface Contamination Discipline
188	
189	**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
190	
191	### Zeroes Theory
192	
193	Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space of the project. The litmus test for any interface decision: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification in the specification.
194	
195	### Rules
196	
197	1. **One canonical form**: Every function, parameter, command, and identifier has exactly one accepted form. Do not accept alternatives.
198	2. **No tolerances**: Do not add `strip_prefix`, case folding, alias acceptance, or alternative parse paths unless the specification explicitly requires temporary backwards compatibility.
199	3. **No undocumented defaults**: Do not add `unwrap_or`, `Option` with silent defaults, or fallback values that expand what callers can omit beyond what the interface contract specifies.
200	4. **No silent normalization**: Do not silently transform input to a canonical form. If input doesn't match the canonical form exactly, reject it.
201	5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and generated content must use the single canonical form — never a tolerated alternative, even if the implementation happens to accept it.
202	
203	### Relationship to Other Disciplines
204	
205	- **String Boundary Discipline** is the serialization-specific instance: one const, exact match, no friendly parsing
206	- **Minting Discipline** is the naming-specific instance: one canonical name per terminal prefix
207	- This section is the general principle those specific disciplines instantiate
208	
209	### Smell Test
210	
211	If you can invoke the same operation two different ways (e.g., `"close"` and `"jjx_close"`), the interface is contaminated. If a function silently accepts `None` where the caller should always provide a value, the interface is contaminated. If documentation shows a shorter form than the canonical one, the documentation is contaminated.
212	
213	## String Boundary Discipline
214	
215	**Core principle**: This is an internal system. Strings that cross serialization boundaries (JSON, display, CLI) are produced and consumed by the same codebase. There are no external users typing variants.
216	
217	### Single Definition Rule
218	
219	Any string value that appears in serialization or display must have exactly **one** const definition. Both parse and emit reference that const.
220	
221	```rust
222	// ✅ Single definition - const is the source of truth
223	const JJRG_STATE_ROUGH: &str = "rough";
224	
225	impl jjrg_PaceState {
226	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
227	        match self {
228	            Self::Rough => JJRG_STATE_ROUGH,
229	            Self::Bridled => JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED,
230	            // ...
231	        }
232	    }
233	
234	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
235	        match s {
236	            JJRG_STATE_ROUGH => Some(Self::Rough),
237	            JJRG_STATE_BRIDLED => Some(Self::Bridled),
238	            // ...
239	            _ => None,  // Bug upstream, not our problem
240	        }
241	    }
242	}
243	```
244	
245	```rust
246	// ❌ String literal appears twice - immature interface
247	impl jjrg_PaceState {
248	    pub fn jjrg_as_str(&self) -> &'static str {
249	        match self {
250	            Self::Rough => "rough",  // First occurrence
251	        }
252	    }
253	
254	    pub fn jjrg_from_str(s: &str) -> Option<Self> {
255	        match s {
256	            "rough" => Some(Self::Rough),  // Second occurrence - drift risk
257	        }
258	    }
259	}
260	```
261	
262	### No Friendly Parsing
263	
264	Do not accommodate input variants. If the canonical form is `"rough"`, do not also accept `"Rough"`, `"ROUGH"`, or `"r"`. Flexible parsing is for external APIs with human users — not internal systems.
265	
266	```rust
267	// ❌ Web-brain: accommodating sloppy input
268	"A" | "a" | "APPROACH" | "approach" | "Approach" => Ok(Self::Approach),
269	
270	// ✅ Internal system: exact match only
271	JJRN_MARKER_APPROACH => Ok(Self::Approach),
272	```
273	
274	If input doesn't match the canonical const exactly, that's a bug in the producer — not a variant to handle gracefully.
275	
276	This applies equally to error diagnostics. Do not add "did you mean?" suggestions, fuzzy matching, or edit-distance scoring to error paths. The producer and consumer are the same codebase — a mismatched string is a bug to fix, not a typo to guess at. List the valid values and stop.
277	
278	### Serde Integration
279	
280	Prefer `#[serde(rename = "...")]` or `#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]` over manual string methods when serde handles both serialization and deserialization. The attribute is the single definition.
281	
282	When manual methods are needed (e.g., for display or CLI), define consts that match the serde representation.
283	
284	### Smell Test
285	
286	If `grep '"rough"'` finds the same string literal in multiple places, the interface is immature. Extract a const.
287	
288	## Constant Discipline (Beyond Strings)
289	
290	String Boundary Discipline covers serialized strings. This section extends to **all** repeated values.
291	
292	### Rule
293	
294	Any magic value that appears 2+ times requires a const. This includes numbers, format strings, and computed values.
295	
296	### Derivation Rule
297	
298	If value B depends on value A, always derive B from A at the use site. Never define B as a separate constant.
299	
300	```rust
301	// ✅ Length derived from the const
302	pub const JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT: &str = "0000000";
303	let short_arg = format!("--short={}", JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len());
304	
305	fn zjjrg_is_commit_sha(s: &str) -> bool {
306	    s.len() == JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT.len() && s.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_hexdigit())
307	}
308	
309	// ❌ Magic number exists separately — drift risk
310	const COMMIT_LEN: usize = 7;  // Could diverge from JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT
311	format!("--short=7")          // Hardcoded, not derived
312	```
313	
314	### Identity Rule
315	
316	The const name must identify the specific value, not just its category. A category-only suffix is ambiguous and becomes a terminal exclusivity violation the moment a second value appears in the same category.
317	
318	```rust
319	// ✅ Suffix identifies the specific command
320	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME_GET_SPEC: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
321	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_BIND: &str = "jjx_bind";
322	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND: &str = "jjx_send";
323	
324	// ❌ Suffix names the role, not the identity — deferred terminal exclusivity debt
325	const JJRGS_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_get_spec";
326	const JJRLG_CMD_NAME: &str = "jjx_bind";  // Blocks JJRLG_CMD_NAME_SEND
327	```
328	
329	### Smell Test
330	
331	If `grep '7'` (or any literal) finds the same value used for the same purpose in multiple places, extract a const and derive.
332	
333	## Constructor Discipline
334	
335	When a struct requires non-trivial initialization (timestamps, captured values, computed defaults), define a constructor helper. All creation sites use the helper.
336	
337	### Rationale
338	
339	Scattered struct construction leads to:
340	- Inconsistent initialization (some sites forget fields)
341	- Duplicated capture logic (timestamps, git SHAs)
342	- Drift when struct fields change
343	
344	### Pattern
345	
346	```rust
347	// ✅ Constructor centralizes initialization logic
348	pub fn jjrg_make_tack(
349	    state: jjrg_PaceState,
350	    text: String,
351	    silks: String,
352	    direction: Option<String>,
353	) -> jjrg_Tack {
354	    jjrg_Tack {
355	        ts: timestamp_full(),           // Captured once
356	        state,
357	        text,
358	        silks,
359	        commit: jjrg_capture_commit_sha(),  // Captured once
360	        direction,
361	    }
362	}
363	
364	// All creation sites use the helper
365	let tack = jjrg_make_tack(PaceState::Rough, text, silks, None);
366	```
367	
368	```rust
369	// ❌ Raw struct construction scattered across files
370	// In jjx_slate:
371	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
372	// In jjx_tally:
373	jjrg_Tack { ts: timestamp_full(), state, text, silks, commit: capture_sha(), direction }
374	// In deserializer:
375	jjrg_Tack { ts, state, text, silks: migrated, commit: UNKNOWN.to_string(), direction }
376	```
377	
378	### When to Use
379	
380	- Struct has fields requiring runtime capture (timestamps, git state)
381	- Struct has fields with non-trivial defaults
382	- Struct is created in 2+ locations
383	
384	### Exception
385	
386	Test files may construct structs directly with test values (no runtime capture needed).
387	
388	## Comment Discipline
389	
390	Comments describe what code does permanently, not why it was written this session.
391	
392	### Prohibited
393	
394	- References to pace names, coronets, firemarks (`₢AEAAI`, `₣AB`)
395	- References to heat names or silks
396	- Phrases like "this migration", "for this pace", "current task"
397	- Explanations of what changed (that's what git commits are for)
398	
399	### Permitted
400	
401	- What the code does in general terms
402	- Why a non-obvious approach was chosen (permanent rationale)
403	- Constraints or invariants the code maintains
404	
405	```rust
406	// ✅ Permanent description
407	// Use unknown commit placeholder for legacy data without commit tracking
408	
409	// ❌ Task-focused — meaningless to future readers
410	// Migration: if tack lacks commit, use JJRG_UNKNOWN_COMMIT for ₢AEAAI
411	
412	// ✅ Permanent rationale
413	// Custom deserializer handles legacy format where silks lived on Pace
414	
415	// ❌ Task-focused
416	// Added in tack-struct-rust-migration pace to support old JSON
417	```
418	
419	## Output Discipline
420	
421	**Core rule**: Every crate must route all output through a declared output module. No naked `println!`, `eprintln!`, `print!`, or `eprint!` anywhere except inside that module's implementation.
422	
423	### Why This Exists
424	
425	Raw print statements scatter output decisions across the codebase. This creates problems ranging from cosmetic (inconsistent format) to fatal (MCP servers use stdout for JSON-RPC — a stray `println!` corrupts the transport). A single output module centralizes the decision of where output goes, what format it takes, and how to disable it.
426	
427	### Sentinel Comment
428	
429	Every file that produces output must carry a standardized import with a sentinel comment:
430	
431	```rust
432	// RCG output discipline: all emission via apcrl_*! — no direct println!/eprintln!
433	use apcd::{apcrl_info_now, apcrl_error_now, apcrl_fatal_now};
434	```
435	
436	The tag `RCG output discipline:` is grep-able. The comment restates the rule at the point where violations would occur. Future editors see the prohibition when they open the file, not buried in a spec.
437	
438	### Four Severity Levels
439	
440	Every output module must provide these four levels with consistent semantics:
441	
442	| Level | Semantics | Continues? | Rate |
443	|-------|-----------|------------|------|
444	| `trace` | Development debugging | Yes | High — may compile out in release |
445	| `info` | Operational milestones | Yes | Low — always present |
446	| `error` | Recoverable failure | Yes | Hopefully rare |
447	| `fatal` | Unrecoverable | No — exits process | Terminal |
448	
449	### Line Discipline
450	
451	Every emission is one complete line. The output module appends the newline — callers never include `\n`. Every emission automatically includes the source file and line number of the call site.
452	
453	Output format:
454	```
455	[LEVEL] [file:line] message text
456	```
457	
458	### Naming Convention
459	
460	All macros carry the project's output module prefix. Three suffix families:
461	
462	**Unconditional (`_now`)** — emit and continue (or exit for fatal):
463	
464	```rust
465	xxx_trace_now!("checking entry {}", i);
466	xxx_info_now!("loaded {} entries", count);
467	xxx_error_now!("parse failed: {}", err);
468	xxx_fatal_now!("config missing, cannot start");  // never returns
469	```
470	
471	The `_now` suffix distinguishes unconditional emission from conditional variants and enables tab completion: `xxx_error_` → `now`, `if`, `eq`, `ne`, ...
472	
473	**Boolean conditional (`_if`)** — evaluates condition, emits if true, returns the bool:
474	
475	```rust
476	if xxx_error_if!(items.is_empty(), "no items loaded from {}", path) {
477	    return default_result;
478	}
479	
480	xxx_fatal_if!(config.is_none(), "config file not found");
481	// only reachable if config is Some
482	```
483	
484	`_if` fuses the condition check and the diagnostic. The caller cannot forget to log when the condition triggers.
485	
486	**Comparison conditional (`_eq`, `_ne`, `_lt`, `_gt`, `_le`, `_ge`)** — compares two expressions, emits if the comparison is true, returns the bool. Uses `stringify!()` to capture expression names and `{:?}` to show values:
487	
488	```rust
489	if xxx_error_ne!(items.len(), expected, "dictionary load") {
490	    return fallback;
491	}
492	// emits: [ERROR] [src/apcrd_dictionaries.rs:47] dictionary load: items.len() (0) != expected (1000)
493	
494	xxx_fatal_lt!(available, required, "insufficient memory");
495	// emits: [FATAL] [src/apcap_main.rs:30] insufficient memory: available (64) < required (256)
496	// then exits
497	```
498	
499	The suffix names the error condition — the thing that is wrong. The output shows both expressions and their values automatically.
500	
501	### Variant Matrix
502	
503	Projects implement the variants they need. The full matrix:
504	
505	| | `_now` | `_if` | `_eq` `_ne` `_lt` `_gt` `_le` `_ge` |
506	|---|---|---|---|
507	| **trace** | unconditional | returns bool | returns bool + stringify |
508	| **info** | unconditional | — | — |
509	| **error** | unconditional | returns bool | returns bool + stringify |
510	| **fatal** | emit + exit | exit or fall through | exit or fall through + stringify |
511	
512	`info` has only `_now` — milestones are unconditional by nature.
513	
514	### What RCG Specifies vs. What Projects Own
515	
516	**RCG specifies:**
517	- The discipline (must have a module, must route through it, must carry the sentinel)
518	- The four severity levels and their semantics
519	- The suffix naming convention (`_now`, `_if`, comparison operators)
520	- Line-oriented output with automatic file/line
521	- No naked print statements
522	
523	**Projects specify:**
524	- The prefix (`apcrl_`, `vvco_`, etc.)
525	- The output target (stdout, stderr, buffer, file)
526	- Trace gating strategy (`cfg!(debug_assertions)`, feature flag, runtime toggle)
527	- Whether the module is macro-based or object-based
528	- Which comparison variants to implement
529	
530	### Relationship to Other Disciplines
531	
532	Output Discipline is an instance of Interface Contamination Discipline applied to program output. Just as there must be one canonical form for each input, there must be one canonical path for each output. A stray `println!` is output contamination — an undocumented emission path that bypasses format, level, and routing decisions.
533	
534	## File Size Discipline
535	
536	Large files degrade code quality by hiding patterns and encouraging copy-paste.
537	
538	### Thresholds
539	
540	| Lines | Action |
541	|-------|--------|
542	| <500 | Good |
543	| 500-800 | Consider splitting by responsibility |
544	| >800 | **Stop and ask** before continuing |
545	
546	### When >800 Lines
547	
548	Do not autonomously refactor. Instead:
549	1. Note the file size in your response
550	2. Identify natural split points (types, serde, validation, operations)
551	3. Ask the user how to proceed
552	
553	### Natural Split Boundaries
554	
555	Large files typically split into 2-3 smaller files by responsibility. Common boundaries:
556	- Data definitions vs. operations on that data
557	- Core logic vs. serialization concerns
558	- Public API vs. internal helpers
559	
560	All split files keep the same cipher prefix for grep-ability.
561	
562	## Specification Authority Discipline
563	
564	**If the specification doesn't call for it, don't build it.**
565	
566	When implementing a module, the specification is the complete description of required behavior. Do not invent features, diagnostics, or affordances beyond what the spec describes — even when they seem "obviously helpful." If you believe a capability is missing from the spec, **stop and ask** rather than silently adding it.
567	
568	### Why This Exists
569	
570	LLM agents have strong instincts toward user-friendliness inherited from web development: fuzzy matching, "did you mean?" suggestions, graceful degradation, helpful error recovery. In an internal system with a perfect protocol, these instincts produce non-load-bearing complexity. The cost isn't just the code — it's the expanded state space (Zeroes Theory) and the false signal that the system tolerates imprecision.
571	
572	### Rules
573	
574	1. **Spec is the authority**: Implement what the spec says. Not more, not less.
575	2. **No speculative features**: Do not add capabilities "because they'll be useful." If they're useful, they'll be specified.
576	3. **Stop and ask on invention**: If you're about to write a non-trivial algorithm (edit distance, scoring heuristics, search strategies, caching layers) that the spec doesn't mention, that's a gate — ask the user whether it belongs.
577	4. **Error paths are not product features**: Error messages should identify the failure and list valid options. They should not attempt to guess the user's intent.
578	
579	### Smell Test
580	
581	If you're writing code that makes the system "nicer" rather than "more correct," stop. Niceness that isn't specified is complexity that isn't earned.
582	
583	## Load-Bearing Complexity
584	
585	Every element in a system must carry weight — its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, the difference must be load-bearing or the code should be homogenized.
586	
587	Rust's type system enforces much of this mechanically: unused fields warn, unused variants warn, dead code warns. The disciplines above — Interface Contamination (no unearned input forms), Specification Authority (no unearned features), Constant Discipline (no unearned literals), Constructor Discipline (no unearned construction sites) — are each instantiations of this principle applied to specific domains. When evaluating a new pattern or extraction, ask: "Is this element load-bearing?" If not, it doesn't belong.
588	
589	## What RCG Does Not Cover
590	
591	Trust Claude's Rust idioms for:
592	
593	- Error handling (`Result<T, E>` patterns)
594	- Documentation (`///` doc comments)
595	- Module organization beyond naming
596	- Struct field design
597	- Lifetime annotations
598	- Generic patterns
599	- Async patterns
600	- Macro design
601	
602	## Quick Reference
603	
604	```rust
605	#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]
606	
607	// Public constants
608	pub const JJRG_DEFAULT_PATH: &str = ".claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json";
609	
610	// Internal constants (z/Z prefix + pub(crate))
611	pub(crate) const ZJJRG_MAX_DEPTH: usize = 16;
612	
613	// Public types
614	pub struct jjrg_Gallops { ... }
615	pub enum jjrg_PaceState { ... }
616	
617	// Internal types (z prefix + pub(crate))
618	pub(crate) struct zjjrg_Output { ... }
619	
620	// Public functions
621	pub fn jjrg_load(path: &Path) -> Result<jjrg_Gallops, String> { ... }
622	
623	// Internal functions (z prefix + pub(crate))
624	pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate(s: &str) -> bool { ... }
625	
626	// Impl methods - also prefixed
627	impl jjrg_Gallops {
628	    pub fn jjrg_new() -> Self { ... }
629	    pub fn jjrg_save(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<(), String> { ... }
630	    pub(crate) fn zjjrg_validate_internal(&self) -> bool { ... }
631	}
632	```
633	
634	## File Templates
635	
636	### Proprietary License Header
637	
638	```rust
639	// Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
640	// All rights reserved.
641	// SPDX-License-Identifier: LicenseRef-Proprietary
642	```
643	
644	### Open Source License Header (Apache 2.0)
645	
646	```rust
647	// Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
648	//
649	// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
650	// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
651	// You may obtain a copy of the License at
652	//
653	//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
654	//
655	// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
656	// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
657	// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
658	// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
659	// limitations under the License.
660	```
661	
662	### Placement
663	
664	The license header goes at the very top of the file, before `//!` doc comments or `#![...]` attributes.
665	
666	## Test File Extraction Checklist
667	
668	When extracting inline tests from `{cipher}r{x}_{name}.rs` to `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs`:
669	
670	### Source File Changes
671	- [ ] Remove entire `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` block
672	- [ ] Change internal functions from `fn` to `pub(crate) fn` if tests call them
673	- [ ] Change internal structs from `struct` to `pub(crate) struct` if tests construct them
674	- [ ] Add `pub(crate)` to struct fields if tests construct the struct
675	
676	### New Test File
677	- [ ] File named `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs` (matching source classifier)
678	- [ ] Copyright header copied from source
679	- [ ] Import: `use super::{cipher}r{x}_{name}::*;`
680	- [ ] Additional imports for types from sibling modules as needed
681	- [ ] Test functions renamed from `test_*` to `{cipher}t{x}_*`
682	- [ ] Each test function has `#[test]` attribute
683	- [ ] NO `#[cfg(test)]` wrapper — lib.rs handles that
684	- [ ] NO `mod tests` wrapper — functions go directly in file
685	- [ ] Helper functions (e.g., `create_test_data()`) can remain unprefixed
686	
687	### lib.rs Wiring
688	- [ ] Add `#[cfg(test)] mod {cipher}t{x}_{name};` declaration
689	- [ ] Place after corresponding `pub mod {cipher}r{x}_{name};`
690	
691	### Verification
692	- [ ] `cargo build` succeeds
693	- [ ] `cargo test` runs all extracted tests
694	- [ ] Test count matches pre-extraction count
695	
696	## Module Maturity Checklist
697	
698	### File Structure
699	- [ ] Source file: `{cipher}r{x}_{name}.rs`
700	- [ ] Test file: `{cipher}t{x}_{name}.rs` (if tests exist)
701	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![deny(warnings)]`
702	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![allow(non_camel_case_types)]` (after deny)
703	- [ ] Every crate root has `#![allow(private_interfaces)]` (after deny)
704	- [ ] `lib.rs` declares source module: `pub mod {cipher}r{x}_{name};`
705	- [ ] `lib.rs` declares test module: `#[cfg(test)] mod {cipher}t{x}_{name};`
706	
707	### Naming Conventions
708	- [ ] All public structs/enums: `{prefix}_PascalName`
709	- [ ] All public functions: `{prefix}_snake_name`
710	- [ ] All public constants: `{PREFIX}_SCREAMING`
711	- [ ] All impl methods: `{prefix}_method_name`
712	- [ ] All internal structs/enums: `z{prefix}_PascalName` with `pub(crate)`
713	- [ ] All internal functions: `z{prefix}_snake_name` with `pub(crate)`
714	- [ ] All internal constants: `Z{PREFIX}_SCREAMING` with `pub(crate)`
715	- [ ] All internal traits/aliases: `z{prefix}_Name` with `pub(crate)`
716	- [ ] All test functions: `{cipher}t{x}_test_name`
717	- [ ] Local variables: normal names (no z-prefix — Rust block scoping suffices)
718	
719	### Visibility Discipline
720	- [ ] Public API uses `pub`
721	- [ ] All internal declarations use `pub(crate)` with `z`/`Z` prefix (every type, not just functions)
722	- [ ] Structs constructed by tests have `pub(crate)` fields
723	- [ ] True private reserved for trivial implementation details
724	
725	### Test Organization
726	- [ ] Tests in separate `{cipher}t{x}_*.rs` files, not inline
727	- [ ] Test file imports source module with `use super::*`
728	- [ ] Test functions prefixed with test file's prefix
729	- [ ] Helper functions local to test file (no prefix needed)
730	
731	### Specification Authority Discipline
732	- [ ] Every function and algorithm traces to a spec requirement
733	- [ ] No "helpful" features invented beyond what the spec describes
734	- [ ] No non-trivial algorithms (edit distance, scoring, fuzzy matching) without spec basis
735	- [ ] Error paths list valid options — they do not guess intent
736	
737	### Interface Contamination Discipline
738	- [ ] Functions accept exactly one canonical form for each parameter — no aliases
739	- [ ] No `strip_prefix`, case folding, or input normalization that expands accepted forms
740	- [ ] No silent defaults that expand what callers can omit beyond the interface contract
741	- [ ] Documentation and examples use the canonical form exclusively
742	- [ ] Zeroes theory applied: no unexplained state space expansion
743	
744	### String Boundary Discipline
745	- [ ] Each serialized/displayed string value has exactly one `const` definition
746	- [ ] `from_str` and `as_str` methods both reference the same const
747	- [ ] No "friendly" parsing variants (no `"A" | "a" | "APPROACH"`)
748	- [ ] `grep` for any string literal finds at most one definition (plus tests)
749	- [ ] Serde attributes used where possible (`rename`, `rename_all`)
750	- [ ] Manual string methods only when serde insufficient (CLI, display)
751	
752	### Constant Discipline
753	- [ ] Magic values appearing 2+ times extracted to const
754	- [ ] Derived values (e.g., length) computed from source const, not hardcoded
755	- [ ] No duplicate literals for same semantic value
756	- [ ] Const names identify the specific value, not just the category (Identity Rule)
757	
758	### Constructor Discipline
759	- [ ] Structs with runtime captures (timestamps, git SHA) have constructor helpers
760	- [ ] All creation sites use the constructor, not raw struct syntax
761	- [ ] Constructor named `{prefix}_make_{type}` or `{prefix}_new`
762	- [ ] Test files exempt (may construct with literal test values)
763	
764	### Comment Discipline
765	- [ ] No references to pace/heat names, coronets, firemarks
766	- [ ] No task-focused language ("this migration", "for this pace")
767	- [ ] Comments describe permanent purpose, not current task
768	
769	### Output Discipline
770	- [ ] Crate has a declared output module (e.g., `{cipher}rl_log.rs`)
771	- [ ] No naked `println!`, `eprintln!`, `print!`, or `eprint!` outside the output module
772	- [ ] Files producing output carry the sentinel comment: `// RCG output discipline: ...`
773	- [ ] Four severity levels implemented: trace, info, error, fatal
774	- [ ] All emissions are line-oriented with automatic file/line
775	- [ ] `_now` suffix on unconditional variants
776	- [ ] `_if` suffix on boolean conditional variants (returns bool)
777	- [ ] Comparison variants (`_eq`, `_ne`, `_lt`, `_gt`, `_le`, `_ge`) stringify arguments
778	- [ ] `fatal` variants exit the process (never return)
779	- [ ] `grep -rn 'println!\|eprintln!\|print!\|eprint!' src/` finds only the output module
780	
781	### File Size Discipline
782	- [ ] File under 500 lines (good)
783	- [ ] File 500-800 lines (noted, consider future split)
784	- [ ] File over 800 lines (**stop and ask** before continuing)
785
